You especially see it it goes back to our cell phone situation. You know when you stop to think about this You have somebody who is going to have a telephone conversation with an important client about an important matter and What what I Did early and would still do today. It's not only would that call be would be scheduled But I would be in an environment That Worked for that call if it was at all possible to do so and so preferably I'm going to be in my office in the place in my office where I Do telephone Which is different than the place in my office where I write Where I do bookkeeping and that person's file is going to be in front of me and maybe I made some notes on a four by six card and I've got a glass of water and you know I'm ready to go and Now we have somebody making that important call Or taking that important call as an interruption and they're taking it on their cell phone While they're peeing at the urinal Well, how effective can they possibly be? They're in the wrong environment and in the wrong place To do that particular job So it's reflecting on What's happening is because they're in the wrong environment often they make the wrong decisions like you're going to get bad results, of course I mean you wouldn't Let's assume it for the sake conversation. Let's assume it's a phone call that has to do with making a sale Well, you and I both sell from the front of the room So would we round up an audience of really good prospects take them in a restroom Pitch them while we pee in front of them You know, that's a pretty bad visual day, but I mean You know or tell them all hey Job along with me as we run through the airport and I'll deliver my sales pitch to you Running backwards as best I can because I got to catch a flight date minutes But that's what people do now with their with the telephone part of their business And let me also say this You know cell phones is oftentimes the every other word playing Yeah, so half the time that you're on the cell phone. It's can you hear me now? Yeah, and I mean that commercial on TV can you hear me now? But I I'm almost embarrassed to call a client on a cell phone just because of that I mean he and I afford to pick up a regular line and call him But again, if you're doing it while you're walking through the parking lot I mean, I've overheard I've been in the supermarket and seen the guy By himself with two toddlers in the cart with him and he's on the cell phone And it's pretty quickly apparent. He's not talking to his wife He is not called home to find out what kind of ketchup to get that goes on a lot by the way But that's a whole nother conversation, but he's on the phone having a business conversation While pushing the shopping cart Getting mustard and dealing with two toddlers Now how how good can this possibly be because your brain is being occupied by other things You're going to do much better if again you put yourself in the right physical and Psychological environment for each important thing that you do Okay, how about we talk about some of the tools that you use in managing yourself and your time I got a list here that I wrote down from all the tools I found throughout all of your works Maybe there's some other ones you want to talk about but first let me just give you the list and we'll talk about them separately The first one was storyboard and then the list itself Daily activity chart schedule conversation planner and facts So let's let's go with first the storyboard. What how does that tool help you manage your time? I'll zip through the list with you one caveat first and that is that my advice to people is to experiment with and find tools that work for them and In different strokes for different folks, there's things that that I see other people use very effectively That don't work for me like an appointment book or a PDA And I see other and vice versa So as you said, this is this is the stuff I use but the most important thing is get some tools find some tools Experiment with some things and find those that work for you that the the Start storyboard I've had them off and on over the years literally up on a wall With one or more walls devoted to them You might want to explain what that is. I think some people might not know what a storyboard is Yeah, there are portable versions too and and and I'm experimenting now with a new portable version That a company called a revenue has but basically the storyboard is It comes from the movie industry and it's how you block out and play in a movie or a television show or a commercial But it's become a planning tool and it came out of Disney for that purpose my thing it's called displayed thinking and Essentially, it's just a piece of corkboard or like I now have in instead of cork I have this big green rolled out sticky thing that you don't need tax for and you use Three by five cards or three or five cards cut in half or four by six cards different sizes and colors and Essentially, you're putting one idea or one task or one piece of a puzzle on each piece of card and then arranging them on the board and But the board allows you to do is easily rearrange and rearrange and rearrange and move things from Do doing done or Al to Mary to Bob It allows you to move it around and it allows you then to see it all In front of you again going back to our whole conversation about how the subconscious works and it works pictorially more than it does anything else and so Simple example if you like our business if you're gonna put on a seminar then you might storyboard the whole seminar And so your vertical columns you might have a column for hotel contract and You might have a column for physical environment and decor and a column for staff and a column for What's good to be sold at the seminar and a column for speakers and A column for meal from it on and on and on and so now under each one of these is You think of something that you need to have in the contract Like I got to remember to have bar stools in the front of the room because I want to sit on a bar stool And they don't normally provide that One little card gets three bar stools written on it and it gets stuck up there on the contract And any time you think of something if you keep some of these cards with you at all times You can jot them down and now you're sticking it up there and gradually put that in order and for example now When it comes time to do the memo About everything that needs to be in the contract It's all arranged up there on the wall in the correct order and you can take down all the cards and go in and type up Or hand them to somebody who types up the memo about the contract in some environments There's more than one person involved in planning a project and this allows everybody to contribute Without having meetings Because I can walk in and look at your storyboard Planning this seminar and know what's going on and I can think oh geez you missed this or hey Maybe we can do that and I could write a little card and stick it up there and it's appropriate position and the portable ones the displayed thinking pads That some people listening to the car will have from Mike from Mike Vance the problem with those are just like lists They're they're better than nothing, but you can't move the stuff around all you're doing is putting ideas in little squares To do what I'm fooling around with that I got from a catalog company called Levent Or is like a vinyl you know like a legal pan holder right, but it's got little pockets in it It stands out cards fit in and so you can move the cards around does it stand up? No At least would but okay because I know both of you and are extremely visual if we file things away They get a lot of us. I mean I can't find anything. It's once it's filed If it's not taped up around my office. I'm lost yeah, and so I you know I use this Sometimes to plan a given project. I have it for the calendar year of all the big stuff and So that's a tool I use I'm a big list guy and again you'll find popular time management a little literature that is anti to-do list And for the life of me I don't get it And everybody I know who gets a lot done and is extremely productive In the entrepreneurial world is a list maker and everybody I've ever Big names. I mean, I coca Spend a little time with him if you read Trump If you read the biography the autobiography I mean everybody's a list maker and everybody I've been around has been a list maker The daily activity chart is about checking off the things that you identify to do in your business every single day And that's really a little accountability device My schedule was a little bit. I mean I've I've pretty much had the same approach to schedule now for about 12 years And then the written material that comes with these tapes I'll stick one in just everybody sees it But my schedule is about 16 months to 18 months at a time and It's it's done on the computer. Yeah, I do know how to use one of the things And it's It's got the phone appointments and the actual it's got everything except the appointments with myself It's got everything but the project work, but it's got phone calls and trips and meetings and Phone appointments and days that are set aside in advance That Vicki then fills up with phone appointments, but I pre-blocked the time And it gets redone about twice a month Conversation planner is simply One of those cards that stuck up on a wall or a page in the legal pad That's kept For each person that I have to have a lot of interaction with So Vicki would be an example Bill Glazer would be an example That particular one Dan has been extremely helpful to me Because you know, I have a number of people just you know that I have to talk to you and instead of you know Racking your brain of oh, I was supposed to tell you know my secretary or my lawyer or something You know, I already have a little note in a little notebook You know with the file folder with them so when I talked to them I just open it up and I learned that from you when I was going through your stuff And so anybody doesn't use that I mean it just saves a ton of time Yeah, we had them in a formal to give credit what credit's due it came from a guy buying them up I'll Larry Dolan and Larry and I created a time management System called test which was an acronym for time effectiveness success training a play on est It's also gives you dating And the conversation planner I got from him and I've used it ever since I mean it allows you as you say to relieve the memory Because otherwise you're sticking stuff in your head and saying man I got to remember the next time I talked to Pete to mention this what makes you tired to try to think like that Yeah, I mean it also You know, it wasn't a problem 20 years ago. It is now And so it relieves the memory and It eliminates the scrap of paper deal where you've got notes What you want to talk that first about 18 different places? and it also is your delegation supervision list Because the thing now so if I if I have a conversation with you about I Know something Paging a retrieving something for me or getting me this piece of information Well, that was on that page and I'll just jot down next to it the date we talked about it and Whatever the action was going to be right at least I'd she'd get this for me by next week Well, it's still on the list the next time I talk to you and it hasn't been checked out or marked through or colored through and So I don't forget to hold you accountable for the stuff I gave to you And it's a big mistake people make when delegating what they tend to do is delegate down the black hole Is they get it out of their hands? Handed to somebody, but then they never checked to see if that somebody actually did it And could you take this a little bit further about you know How it's important it is to teach your staff to have one of these so they don't interrupt you 12 times a day Well, that's sure that's part of I mean, you know, we could do a whole lot of program But yeah, it's part of now training the people who interact with you be that staff vendor associates even clients That this is the way we work and you don't email me 16 times a day every time you have a brain fart with 16 different questions Or you don't parade in and out of my office all day long You don't cost me again at a time and place where I'm trying to do something else And so, you know the same cell phone problem we talked about is the hey, can I have a minute? I got a quick question for you in the cafeteria while you're trying to eat lunch And instead you cluster your questions and topics and Then you meet with me or talk with me on a scheduled basis Or you send one email that has the 12 items in it not to me of course because I don't get it at all But I mean if you were using email And so yeah these tools Just has whatever ones that Reflect the discipline that you're using you now got to somehow get everybody around you On the same page if not using the precisely the same tool using at least precisely the same method And then last on your list was was was facts which You know, it's one of the two pieces of equipment ever invented. I like And I rely on it a lot for both inbound and outbound So with all these tools when do you schedule and actually plan your day? Well a lot of mine are off the table Weeks and in some cases even months in advance So for example this year, it's very easy to plan the days in May because there's already no time left to play I've already heard you lament about that. You've over scheduled yourself for May So so may is going to be pretty easy From here on out because there's no time But so in many cases they're off the table or large portions of them are off the table Weeks and even months in advance I tend to revisit my schedule before each week and Then I do the actual planning of what I'm going to do today at the end of the day or evening yesterday so that I Hit the ground running in the morning with the day totally planned and And I'm pretty precise. I mean I'm allocating time slots. I mean today, of course We had this from three to six But I started With an hour of writing Working on one of my books from 6 30 to 7 30 and then I had I don't remember now, but 13 or 14 things that had to be done before I left for a 10-15 appointment and they ranged from you know one quick answer to a fax to get in a contract down or whatever and They were arranged in priority order At the end of the day yesterday Sitting there to be Tackled and I got all but one of them done so that's not a bad ratio today And then I had an appointment and and so it's planned The night before and I do it for a practical reason so I hit the ground running and I do it for a psychological reason so that the subconscious knows what we're going to be doing and And and to some extent gets to do some work in advance while I sleep And of course I write so that's a direct application But it doesn't matter whether it would be your first meeting of the day or your first phone Conversation of the day whatever it is that you were doing if the subconscious is pretty clear on what's going to be going on It will have done some preparation Long before you were actually doing it physically and then some percentage of what you're doing becomes deja vu And it happens then much quicker and much more efficiently and much more effectively And one of the things that you do that practically nobody else that I know does is like when you call me You'll actually say Lee. I only have 12 minutes Yeah, and I even do it with So with friends because I've conditioned myself to do it with everybody And so I know if there's something I need to ask you or tell you I got 12 minutes and you're right on the button Hey at the end of 12 minutes, you're gone Yeah, well in many cases I have to be and the subconscious also has a pretty good clock But yeah, I do that now almost it's not quite has Sort of every time built in as turning the button on the overhead projector on and off Fast whereas I do that Now every time and if I want to not do it it takes effort But this is close. I mean I usually People if they've had phone appointment scheduled they've been told and I still will start the conversation that way and And pretty much do it all the time. You're right and I've learned that from you and it's been it's a way of getting to the point You know, I live in the south and you know a lot of times here in Virginia People want to shoot the breeze, you know, how are you? I'm fine. How are you and you know, it's like, okay? That's nice. Let's move along Yeah, it's very interesting, you know in my coaching programs In Gold VIP and Platinum Plus the months that we don't have meetings they have 15 minute actually 14 minute private calls with me And they're pre-scheduled so If Al Schwartz is in coaching Al has the same he's got from 10 15 to 10 30 every month on Pointed day and he actually remembers it. Well, we remind But they're actually 14 minute time slots because they're back to back and so the 15 minutes the 15th minute is hang up on one and answer the next one and Most people, you know when they start with being those programs can't conceive of how you can get anything done in 15 minutes and Many of them it's really trained them and Conditioned them to be much more effective in the way they communicate with people on the phone And so not only are they getting a lot done with me in 15 minutes But they're getting a lot done with everybody else in 15 minutes and they know because they're back to back I mean at 14 minutes. I don't we could be in mid-sentence Talking about impending Armageddon and I'm hanging up on You missed your opportunity and so a lot of them have gotten so that just like me they they kind of They've planned they got there three things they want to talk about in priority order They have they know that this one is going to take four minutes and this one's going to take seven minutes They have a little clock working in their head and a number of them have gotten very very very good at it And you know with any work Predetermining how long it should take Is a big step to getting it done in the time it should take Most people approach all tasks that's even the fallacy with the to-do list I mean if you've got a to-do list with 12 items on it, but you haven't predetermined How much time each one of those 12 things is going to get and it's supposed to take I guarantee you you're winding up at the end Of the day with a whole bunch of items left undone on the to-do list And folks he's not kidding about this on my notes He we have talked about some of these questions. I said here some of the questions I'm going to ask you and he has actually written down there four minutes for this six minutes for this He's very serious about time management All right, here's a very important question and that is how do you keep unplanned? Situations, you know other people from throwing you off schedule You know people showing up at the door or workman that you weren't planning on or or you know someone in the office You know having a crisis How long the guy used to play for the Raiders? You're not a football fan, but football fans would know him and he's on you might know him because he's on the Fox show on Sunday mornings during football season He's next to Terry back that's right. Anyway, how he was a very physical player violent and How he was asked when his daughter be Dating how he was gonna you know keep her from having problems and he said the first boy the first Guy that shows up the door. I'm gonna shoot him and I figure the word will get around I Would take there's almost none of these problems that a bullet won't do So, you know other people throwing off your schedule, I mean one part of that answer is How much of a cute tolerate and sanction and make exceptions for versus How tough you are on repelling it? And so the whole thing in the no BS time management book about time vampires, I mean that you know that person who's Showing up the door with I only need a minute to ask you this quick question if you let them do it You're sanctioning the behavior and they'll continue to do it and you got to be Enough of an SOB to say no, I don't even have a minute go away We we have a scheduled time to talk tomorrow at three o'clock put it on the list and And And you know people thought well it took you as long to say that is it whatever yeah, but The behavior continues and pretty soon they're at your door three times four times five times a day And so that's part of it the The other thing is that Kissinger had this great line that And keep in mind this is Kissinger at the Nixon White House During water date He said there could be no more crisis this week Because I'm fully booked That's good, and you know if you're fully booked Then no one can throw off your schedule Because there's no schedule left to throw off. I mean time can be wasted or abused only Only if it's available to be wasted or abused and So what supply doesn't exist? Then the waste and the abuse literally can't occur and You know it's the if the person showed up at your door and said yeah, I only need a minute or two to ask you a question and In 30 seconds you had a telephone appointment with now you pick Whoever you view is extremely important Your number one client who pays you a fortune every year or the president of the United States or the new pope Or Your divorce attorney or you know who whoever it is who who's like at this current moment is The VIP of all VIPs in your life of critical importance to you And you know they were calling in 30 seconds and you were waiting for their call and the guy showed up the door It's that I need two or three minutes. Would you brush them off? Yes Well, so why isn't the appointment you have with yourself to do X? Every bit as valuable and important to be safeguarded as is the appointment you would have with the pope or the president and When you view it that way you get to be kind of tough about this And so, you know my days are You ask about the the planning and it's usually it's the night before or the afternoon before but my days actually aren't Aren't scheduled in the sense that you've got a page in the appointment book and you got our meeting at three o'clock you got lunch at 11 And a couple of phone calls written in My days are scripted And the difference is that the whole day is scripted and so But 13 or 14 things that I did this morning Before I left for a 10-15 appointment. They were all Listed with the number of minutes it was going to take to do them assigned to them totaling up to the number of minutes in the time block made available to them and and Consequently any interruption any just because every one of the minutes was was was allocated There was no unallocated minutes So if I let anything disrupt or interrupt that Something in that script isn't going to happen There's a direct and clear trade-off Something or someone is going to get shorted or cheated today Or bumped to the back of the line if I let somebody bump to the front of the line So my days are scripted in some cases to the minute and certainly in little hunks of minutes and I know Just as giving a speech or a seminar And I don't know if you do it to the degree that I do or not, but I mean if I've got 60 overheads for the day the Sheet behind every overhead has the time on it of where I'm supposed to be When I hit that overhead right so if I'm supposed if it says 210 And it's 230. I know I'm in trouble If it says 210 and it's 130 I know I'm going to come up short and so I'm constantly Assessing and making little adjustments to stay on schedule So that you don't have a seminar day where you have three hours of material left over Or you're supposed to end at five o'clock in a 230 you say oh shit we're out of material I guess everybody leaves early and And I mean and I do the same thing with my day so I'm constantly assessing and Adjusting if necessary Where I am on the work list against where we are on the clock And so For most days there is no time left to be wasted or abused because I have pre-assigned it all If not before this then at least the day before And as you say all my appointments have end times So the phone appointments are booked with end times So and it's amazing how people have to be trained to get this But they get it Clients and vendors alike my doctor my attorney my CPA They've all the first couple of times they haven't got it You know Vicki has booked a ten o'clock appointment for them and told them the appointment is 10 to 10 15 And at 10 15 they are like still talking and I'm going sorry it's over because I got a 10 18 and We didn't get what we needed to cover it So you need to go back to Vicki and get another phone appointment scheduled and it's probably gonna be about three weeks from now So I know I gotta go now teach them now But some of them are pretty dull about this and it's taken three or four times You know for them to get it, but then they get it and now that they got it Everything works great with each one of them and again as you said I typically reminded it started the call to the way now Glad we're on the phone and we're on the phone five minutes late because this was a 10 o'clock appointment And it's now 10 05 and it's over a 10 15 so that means we got 10 minutes Not and my personal in-person appointments are the same Be it meeting with one person or meeting with five person Everybody like knows ahead of time Here's how long the meeting is not we're gonna be here You know forever Until the skin is falling off of our skeleton And and the project and work appointments that I set with myself are the same they have in times and see my belief is Again, it's sort of linked to my direct response work. You know one of the things that makes That is the distinction between direct response advertising and normal advertising is an offer and The further distinction within the existence of an offer is a deadline and so true direct response people will always tell you if there's no Deadline if there's no expiration date. There is no offer Similarly to me if there's no end time it's not an appointment So most people think an appointment has a beginning time and no end and Consequently, they're not they're not going to be exceptionally productive To me it's not an appointment unless it has a start time and an end time Well Since we've been talking about you know keeping unplanned situations, you know people throwing you off from schedule But let's talk about you know real emergencies and real crises You know somebody's been in a car accident Well, you know you don't everybody pretty much knows my house on fire story from Marketing speech. I mean I was pretty reluctant to let that interfere with my telephone Yeah, what the first thing about quote-quote real emergencies and real crises and Again, I was using the same methods that I'm using now at times when I was involved in businesses where crisis and emergency was You know the norm The first thing is most people operate with an overly broad definition of emergency and an overly broad definition of crisis, yeah, and You know in a renegade millionaire system and in some of my other stuff I mean I say you know the big principle is that nothing is ever as bad Or incidentally as good Is it first appears to be and the corollary to that is is that very few things are as urgent as they first appear Or has other people Think that they are And so I think you start by being more Critical about what is an emergency and what isn't an emergency and what is a crisis and what is not a crisis I told To the super conference. I told the story of the guy trying to get to John Wayne in his office Badgering his secretary and badgering his secretary was call after call after call You know, she's finally interrupting him and accosting him and saying so and so from the studio is called back out every hour And he says it's urgent and John Wayne's answer was well the next time he calls back Ask him who it's urgent for him or me. I think we're so ahead. Yeah, it's a terrific story because You know just because it's urgent to them Really mean it's urgent to you. I tell people Often told the story and usually the women in the audience cringe over this story by the way But and I just do the shortest version possible, but For a number of years when I was kid my parents were in the amway business my father's preset schedule was Three days a week for three hours in the afternoon. He went to his telephone desk in the basement office and made his customer telephone calls and Will be the person who? Opened that door marks down steps and interfered with him and those three hours it was just not an advisable thing to do and I was in the barn Got my arm between a wall and a horse's ass And was dumb enough to try and push the horses, but by bracing my elbow against the wall I dare and snap the rest And I go into the house with this thing bleeding and you know some of it literally pierced through the skin and My mother who does not do who never drove a car is you know beside herself and He's on her way to get dead to and he were about halfway through his three-hour time lock And I'm saying no no no no no you know just let's get a rag some ice do something But we only got about an hour and a half and he'll emerge I'll wait Because the wrist and the pain of the wrist was nothing compared to the pain waiting for disturbing him which I knew But she insisted and he came up and looked and said put a rag and some ice on it and I'll be finished in an hour and then we'll go to the hospital and People are you know again? I usually see women mostly cringing they hear this story But of course if you stop to think about it, what happens when you get to the hospital and you go to the emergency room with a broken wrist They put ice on it and they wrap it in a in a rag and I think we sat there for four hours and some odd minutes before anybody saw us I imagine today. It's at least that bad if not worse in most emergency rooms And so he was absolutely correct Now he wouldn't have been obviously if you know I'd had my head stomped in and walked in the house with one eyeball and One testicle in my hand But I mean the broken wrist is a broken wrist, you know And and I mean he's absolutely or was absolutely Correct and and I never forgot the lesson You know, it's a real mistake To let yourself be stampeded Whether that's into a decision or into an action or into setting aside your your days script in favor of someone else's emergency urgency or crisis Without Really determining that there is no other option I Was I was running a company that was in financial crisis like every minute General cassette. Yeah, and so you do by the way sort of become a little immune to it for a period of time I mean, I think in a 24 month period of time. I believe we had a positive checking account balance for two days What a stressful experience and There was a day when at 10 o'clock in the morning Our only vendor I Started to say main vendor for the Doubterly got these things on your bookshelf and We're pretty much we like gotta have these so we can't produce anything and this vendor to whom we were both fully behind in paying and It had over the last month a couple NSF checks For good reason the president of this company had finally just Apparently lost his patience and he let our cop troll know that we were cut off and Not only couldn't we get anything on credit we couldn't get anything like cash until we paid the entire Outstanding balance and then we were going to be on cash And the Comptroller is Immediately, I don't remember what I was doing at the time, but I know that whatever it was was interrupted by the Comptroller in my office having a fit because This meant that the world was over and you know the cataclysm had occurred and we might as well burn the building down and send everybody home because life on this planet is over and At the time, you know, I said hey the rest of the day is planned here You know I got stuff to do and nothing I do at the moment is going to change this So go go about your business. I'll go about my business and at the end of the day I got time at six o'clock to figure out what to do about this and And I know he wasn't just frustrated beyond all belief he wasn't around much longer And at the end of the day I figured out what to do about it and I did treat it as a relative emergency I rearranged my next day got on an airplane and flew to Minneapolis and was waiting for the guy when he got to his office to negotiate but There was nothing I was going to do that minute and And it was probably better to let the guy cool anyway. And so, you know this whole Somebody appears in front of you Physically or by phone or fax or email or Picture they send out to your cell phone any of the other Electronic devices that they communicate with and try and stampede you into Setting aside your entire script and all your intentions for the day and Jumping into theirs and that really is what it's about Is it's going to be yours or it's going to be there? And you're going to defend your turf And get everybody to function the way you want them to function or you're going to have no turf And there's not much room in between You know Dan I've watched you over the years and you can even Stay very super productive even when going through You know like when you were recovering from your own divorce a few years ago and even when you had a lot of financial problems I guess the question is how do you stick to those objectives and plans even when you do feel Overwhelmed with personal problems because you know a lot of people allow their personal problems to take precedent over their business It's like everything has to fall apart because Something has gone wrong in their personal life You know, I think there's a couple of things I think first of all if you are a person of reasonable integrity And you have made commitments Then you keep those commitments pretty much regardless of what's going on and You know personal family Problems are certainly the most difficult and the most challenging that anyone faces But one of the prices of being an entrepreneur that everybody in the entrepreneurs life does need to understand is There is nobody for us to call and take personal days and and the clients the customers the people at the other end of our process Who rely on us in some cases who we have literal contractual relationships with not just not just handshake commitments They've got to be served regardless and So again the less the less unscripted unscheduled uncommitted time you have the less of a Big amount of angst How to deal with this is because you have no choice but to deal with it Never sure has this has this quote. I'm not going to get it right now. It's in one of my books that Even on the morning after his wife's death the farmer must still milk the cows and That's part of being an entrepreneur Given that then the most important skill I think is And again, I got it about Psycho-cypsobrenetics or other sources other people use different terminology, but is to compartmentalize and so I Literally have a little you know kind of long little lockers in my head and so whatever Each thing has its own little box and Whatever the situation is it can be put away in that box and the door closed and Open the next box and totally focus on that box And go back and open the other box and so you essentially Sort of schedule your misery just like you would schedule anything else In the In the Autobiography that seems to be the project from hell first kidding it written now Every vendor involved seems to have lost their mind in the last three weeks, but I talk about our Suicide in several different ways and you know, there's a there's a line somebody said as well You know, you can always kill yourself later And you know they're right about that It's not so you can be miserable later You can open the box of the personal angst of illness or divorce or financial disaster or whatever pretty much any time And go in there and pull around in it. You don't need to let it Spill out over everything and take control any more than you let anything else spill out all over everything and take control But all that is a conscious decision that you've made about your life And a lot of people have never never been you know, their parents have never taught them this society doesn't tell anybody This is acceptable to do so this is Society teaches exact opposite Exactly. I mean you're almost a bad guy. Yeah, if you don't fall apart when somebody else is having a crisis Well, you know, I guess part of it that I got the cow thing You know when I was a kid we were a lot of race And just like the cow, you know, the horses have to be fed twice a day and the horses stalls have to be clean And they have to be taken care of and it doesn't really much matter whether you're sick or somebody in your family is sick Or your house burned down the night before or your truck was repossessed or your wife left you or it's a country-restored song and all those things happen You've done this joke There you go now the The you know the horse starts to be fed and he has to be fed at 7 o'clock in the morning and I mean so like I got that and I Mean, I think it's a matter of you know integrity commitments to others and commitments to yourself that if you are That that if you are going to declare yourself an entrepreneur and you are going to enter into relationships where others rely on you Be they your employees or your or your vendors or your clients or your banker You know then then then you've got to face the fact that you live differently then your wife's brother Who's an executive at Motorola and can call in sick and has 14 personal days a year And if you're not willing to do that then by all means hang up the entrepreneur sign Before you get yourself in a position where you really do some damage and go get a job Well, I know both of us as speakers, you know, we've talked many times. It doesn't make any difference if we're sick We still go or you know something you happen death and family death and family with both of us or somebody's dying We still go because that's our integrity. We've given our word and There are no days off and I find that on a subconscious level because I don't have time to be sick that Even if I felt bad by the time I get there. I'm really not that sick anymore Absolutely because my my subconscious knows you can't be sick. Well, see I you know, I do believe I mean you and I both believe that essentially the way people Think what goes on inside their heads? The vacuums that exist in their life Have a great deal of do with what's attracted into it and a lot of people attract drama and crisis and emergency and tragedy because they have room for it and Again, if you make the dead air go away and There's no room for it then kissing your line becomes real And you tend not to attract it into your life because there's no place to put it Most people just have too much space right Well, let's talk about your daily regimen. I know one of the things you do every day, you know I know there's one thing you do every single day What are some of the other things you do and and and you do them? I've had as many as eight I Have less When you do every day by the way is right. Yeah, okay. Yeah, I mean specific to talk about business That I'm down to two that that that occur without deviation Every single day except when on true vacation When I mean a total not work day, but every single work day and that includes Christmas No Although I do I usually write on Christmas morning because in every environment I've been in I'm up before everybody else even the kids but But yeah, one is I write and specifically I write for one hour every day for my Projects because I do a lot of copywriting for clients and if I did not do that the client work would totally take over And I would never write anything for myself So even this morning for example, and I'd leave in two days on a little trip and this week's very time-pressured But some things that certainly could have been Justified being on an a list and could have sucked up that other hour this morning Are gonna wait till next week because that hour is blocked out beforehand And then there's only so much other time available to assign so every morning And on the rare times I don't do it first thing in the morning I plug it in some other time during the day, but Pretty much every morning and certainly every day. There's an hour of writing on my books and my information products and And and the other thing that still Wingers is Doing at least one proactive thing every day to create future business Which is reaching the point for me that it's laughable, but still I Won't take that one off the daily activity chart until I'm ready to do no business So every single day even on the day for example like tomorrow is a consulting day So I'll be with a client from 8 30 in the morning Until 4 o'clock in the afternoon I'll write from From 7 to 8 and then go get But when we finish at 4 I will go do something some one thing respond to a fax Give somebody a media contact to follow up on Do something that crimes the pump for future business That is not about the present-day dollar, but it's about a future down And so those are the two things And as I say at one time there was eight, so I really don't need a chart anymore to check these off the because I'm down to two but and And I've been pretty Whatever you want to say dogmatic religious about both of those for well over a decade and a half and Consequently, you know a book gets done every year where some people are still trying to get the book done that they started 20 years ago and the newsletters get done every month And pretty much get done on time and and the pipeline stays full And there's never been you know a shortage of business sort of correlated to this is I you know I've long refused never to have even in those times of extreme crisis or emergency Never to have a totally unproductive day Unless again, it's a tote. It's a designated non-workday. It's a goof off day But but I you know if it's midnight And it's been unproductive. I'll still I'll go down there at five minutes to midnight and get something That I view as productive within my definitions of productivity done before the days end Oh By the way, Dan on February 24th in 2005 you sent me or Vicky sent me What is called the revised Kennedy schedule abbreviated the schedule goes all the way through December 29th and Literally folks on this schedule that he sent me and the reason he does it because you know, you know do some work together One I know not to call you on certain days I know where you are and I'm sure this saves you a ton of time with you know people who are in your circle Who need to know where you are? Saves me time saves them time Saves other people a lot of frustration When you totally take days or blocks of time off of the calendar And people know it Then they're not you know bugged gee I called I left the message I faxed you and it's three days have gone by and I haven't heard from you And they're like cotton bothered But if they know That entire week you're not looking at anything And so you actually get my schedule schedule some clients get what we call a schedule advisory every so often that is not as as detailed as what you've seen but essentially gives them warnings of Time periods when I'm going to be in communicado and not looking at anything right? I mean there's a little line here I will be in communicado 37 through 325 advise clients there will be no responses to inbound communications from me during this period I mean that's right here on your yeah less and better that they only know Well, you're the only person I've ever known to do this and it's been I always have it tacked up here my 2c bulletin board because I know if I need to get in touch with you or I want to talk to you All I have to go do is look at this and I know it Approximately, you know where you are Yeah, and we you know I've and of course you go back to that time when I was traveling a lot. I mean we It was far worse than it is now Where there would be extended periods that everything was going to stack up and I always made a practice of continually advising people both to Avoid them being frustrated to get them to encourage them to get their button gear and deal with things beforehand and To reduce the stress on me and on staff Them having unrealistic expectations of when they were going to be communicated You know Peter Drucker wrote about distinct distinctions distinctions between efficiency and effective How do you draw that line? Well Drucker's work all those slow-going courses well worth reading But in this context You know efficiency is has to speed and getting something done in the shortest possible time And and effectiveness is of course getting the best possible results and sometimes they go together and sometimes they don't and So for example, you know, I would argue that the way I work was scheduled phone appointments is both. It's efficient and it's effective It's efficient for both parties completely eliminates phone tag And and it's and it's efficient because every phone appointment as an end time And it's effective because I'm in Not totally without exception, but for the most part I am in an appropriate place an appropriate environment To deal with the telephone appointments, I've had an opportunity to prepare and I have The information in front of me that I need to make those calls And to respond to what's going to come up on those calls. So it's it's efficient and effective Email the way most people use it is extremely efficient and for the most part extremely ineffective It's efficient because it's quick and cheap You don't have to go put a piece of paper in a fax machine. You don't have to address an envelope You don't have to fill out a FedEx air bill You just type and push a button and Some people the real sophisticated techno geeks can dictate directly into the thing and so they can talk and push a button And so it's extremely efficient for the most part. It's extremely ineffective It's it's the least Impactful form of written communication arriving at the other end In fact, I was listening to talk radio lives coming back from Pointment in JD Hayworth whose congressman from Phoenix Well, I like this guest hosting on show he said that In his office He was talking about communicating with your congressman. He said in his office Faxes have the most weight Phone calls have the second most weight and email has the least weight and in fact for the most part the email never gets looked at by him and He especially dislikes it because people do it quickly without giving it much thought In some cases he'll get three or four emails from the same person Even in the same day and now you'd have to stitch those together to get the person's complete thought and the faxes You can scoop up and take out with him shum in a briefcase work with about an airplane and make notes on them without anybody or the staff having to download or transcribe anything for and So, you know for all of for all sorts of reasons including now spam filters and stuff like it for it's an extremely ineffective means of communication from the standpoint of impact from the standpoint of effectiveness for The person at the sending and receiving in for you or me It's ineffective because it opens the doors to too much junk communication badly communication What I call brain parts, you know the person thinks oh here's an idea. Here's a question. Let's send Lee an email And there it goes and you now you know your thing says you've got mail and you've got 520 pieces of mail And so it's not an effective Communication tool and it's becoming less effective with each passing day, but it is efficient and so I think you have to like take everything you do and every tool you use and every procedure you have and every Rule that you impose and you have to have some standards you have to hold it up and evaluate it and one of them is You know is it efficient, but am I trading off effectiveness for efficiency? Is it? Is it haste makes waste rather than? productive haste And and is it something I should be doing at all is it something I should allow in my world You know at all and most people don't think in terms of sort of definitional standards and so you know it for example we talk about productivity the entire time and If you ever want to have some fun with people in a coaching environment or or a seminar or 101 consultation you start to ask them definition questions Like you want to be more productive. Well, what is your definition of productive? Just like someone say well, I want to be rich while it's defined that for me I don't know and for the most part they haven't thought through a definition And so they're trying to be more productive without having to find what productivity is and that's I call that playing blind art You know you're running around shooting arrows at no target And you might get it You know but but the odds aren't real good In there's some guru out there saying shoot and then aim. Yeah, and that's Tom Peters, isn't it? Yeah Yeah, and And it used to be a joke and now people are teaching it. I mean I don't get that at all Shoot and then aim, you know what when I when I was getting my Arms racing license to drive in real races at the real racetrack. You have to drive in a bunch of county fairs and The county fair tracks are in many cases. The thing is only functional for the week the fairs there They stick up like temporary posts. I mean, it's really a pretty ugly chaotic environment and One of the things about it is you never like quite Know where they're gonna put the finish line and I quickly discovered it was a good idea when you rock their warm and a horse up To make sure that you've got a fix on where the damn finish line was That makes sense otherwise you could like stop short you could stop driving and It's never made any sense to me to be trying to hit an invisible target And so, you know if people do create definitions for themselves then they have The equivalent of a litmus test, you know, they have the ability to hold something up to it And see, you know, does this pass the test? I mean I've long had my productivity definition as the use of your time-talent Energy and resources in a way that moves you measurably closer to your meaningful goals And so you can tell whether your goals the thing you're going to do for the next 12 minutes is truly productive or not now measuring it against that definition You know Dan in some of your writings you've said that the ultimate liberation for the entrepreneur isn't delegation But it's actually replacement Explain to us what you mean by that I've got it written down somewhere that came off of a t-shirt or a bumper sticker That that I'm trying to live my life without being there That's pretty good. Yeah and That really is What the entrepreneur ought to be about especially by sort of nature And conditioning most entrepreneurs aren't really good managers and so Delegation is sort of done out of necessity And and replacement is done more out of strategy and accomplishment and so that's literally went Tim Varga who some of the people on the call will know Ken's built and Sold a lot of successful companies and run a lot of successful companies in the largest company in the country ensuring nurses for example And can't always says that if I start a business and it still requires my attention on a day-to-day basis 36 months later. It's not a business. It's a job And you know he's about got it right and so You really want to aspire to be moving in the direction of and strategically developing your business so that In place after place after place in function after function after function you get to literally replace yourself not just Delegate work that you then must supervise on a day-to-day basis and when you do it You gotta get out of the way You can't be You can't be the ghost of supervision past You know hanging over the person's shoulder and micromanaging what they do Replacement really has to be replacement In the relationships that I have that constitute that for me I Keep in mind the political term of advise and consent And so I try and advise and consent not supervise Not metal and not micromanage And it wasn't easy to to to learn But you know I've gotten good at that too So there are a number of relationships and people sometimes puzzled by it I'll get weird looks sometimes for example with the horse racing business because we'll buy I mean we'll spend serious money and we'll buy horses I mean I my trainer and some of the horses partner will come to me and say here's you know five We're looking at and here's the story on each one and I'll ask questions And I may raise questions And ultimately I will always end the conversation with and do what you think best and I'll be out of the way And I make it a point never to second guess That decision because if I'm gonna second guess it or I'm gonna micromanage it Then it's unfair to the other person and I really ought to just go do it myself Well that sort ties in with my next question which is Is your good enough good enough Part of this effectiveness and efficiency discussion You know when when can you get into your mind that good enough is good enough You know I can now call creative hold to this and move on I think the That you from a time standpoint from productivity standpoint you've got to face And you've got to be very situational about it And so you know just for example Material I'm getting ready to package up and deliver by FedEx to a New client who's getting work product from me for the first time I'm going to be A little more careful about Its appearance and its organization and The quantity of typos And all of that that I am with the client I've been working with for three years who Who who is now? Who gets me? Who we have sort of a rhythm and Who is likely to be more forgiving? Because he's gotten good results and gotten good results and now it's about the results It's not about the fancy folder that it comes in and But that may make a 15 minute difference to me today of getting something out the door in 15 minutes to me is a lot so Certain information products, you know, I mean the product that is good to go on a bookstore shelf And be looked at by media reviewers and By other publishers who are going to make decisions about the next book deal Has to be edited a lot more carefully than the self-published book that is only going to go to My own raving fans and is never going to sit on a shelf It is never going to be seen by the book critic at Time magazine It is not going to have any impact one way or the other on my next commercial book And so, you know good enough is good enough one pass Some sales letters are first draft some sales letters are 20 drafts and they can't all be 20 drafts Or they shouldn't be anyway, you know, if you you know if you're going to have a lot of output so I'm glad perfectionism versus situational perfectionism just absolutely kills our productivity well We were talking about the tools that you use in managing yourself and your time and the last one was about the facts and you've You've talked a lot about that already, but just tell us why you've just so in love with the facts Well, I mean it has the impact of It has the impact of it. It has the immediacy of A telephone call or an email, but it's a more manageable document From my standpoint, it's infinitely more manageable. Nobody has to download it nobody has to transcribe for me I get it the way it was sent At the other end it has more impact And At the other end has communication comes to me it forces a person to think before they communicate See if you take their inbound calls Most people jump on the phone to call you at impulse without having thought through the conversation And in many cases the conversation can't be completed because they didn't think it through so now one conversation is going to be two conversations Or worse The email we've already talked about the fact seems to slow them down When they are going to put it on a piece of paper they treat it like a letter But it's quicker So I still think it's the best form of business to business vendor to client form of communication that there is Okay, you know since you're Let's say I know you work more than a six-hour day But let's just take a six-hour day is more productive than most people's 40-hour weeks Because of the control you use let's just talk a little bit how you personally work So let's just take a day on how you personally work and just sort of run through it You know for our listeners so they get sort of a you know a visual of the Dan Kennedy in action First thing I do differently a lot of people's like You get up very early by the way Things are Again, you know I exercise a great deal of control over how people communicate with me and How I communicate with other people so that there's not constant disruption in distraction and Interruption I mean I use the facts more than any other method. I do not own a cell phone This is the end of this CD go on now to the next CD
